coredata.dc:title: A sensitive turn on fluorescent probe for detection of biothiols using MnO2@carbon dots nanocomposites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Garg, Dimple
Mehta, Akansha
Mishra, Amit
Basu, Soumen
coredata.dc:description: Presently, the combination of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and metal oxide nanostructures in one frame are being considered for the sensing of purine compounds. In this work, a combined system of CQDs and MnO2 nanostructures was used for the detection of anticancer drugs, 6-Thioguanine (6-TG) and 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP). The CQDs were synthesized through microwave synthesizer and the MnO2 nanostructures (nanoflowers and nanosheets) were synthesized using facile hydrothermal technique. The CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence emission at 420nm when excited at 320nm wavelength. By combining CQDs and MnO2 nanostructures, quenching of fluorescence was observed which was attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, where CQDs act as electron donor and MnO2 act as acceptor. This fluorescence quenching behaviour disappeared on the addition of 6-TG and 6-MP due to the formation of Mn-S bond. The detection limit for 6-TG (0.015μM) and 6-MP (0.014μM) was achieved with the linear range of concentration (0–50μM) using both MnO2 nanoflowers and nanosheets. Moreover, the as-prepared fluorescence-sensing technique was successfully employed for the detection of bio-thiol group in enapril drug. Thus a facile, cost-effective and benign chemistry approach for biomolecule detection was designed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Turn on fluorescence
MnO2 nanostructures
Biomolecule detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MnO2 NS and NF
Characterization of CQDs and MnO2 NF and NS
Fluorescence Turn-off of CQDs by MnO2 Nanostructures
Kinetic Study (Testing at Different Time Intervals)
Mechanism for Fluorescence Turn on Detection of 6-TG and 6-MP by CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Visible spectrum of CQDs, MnO2 NF@CQDs, MnO2 NS@CQDs, MnO2 NF, MnO2 NS; (b) emission spectrum of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
DLS spectra for (a) CQDs, (b) MnO2 NF@CQDs, (c) MnO2 NS@CQDs and (d) emission spectrum of CQDs and MnO2 NF@CQDs and MnO2 NS@CQDs.
(a) XPS survey of CQDs, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s spectrum of CQDs and (d) XRD pattern of MnO2 NF/NS.
(a) BET N2 adsorption–desorption curves for MnO2 NF and NS, (b) and (c) HRTEM image of MnO2 NS, (d) HRTEM image of MnO2 NF.
Elemental mapping of MnO2 NF@CQDs for (a) C, (b) O and (c) Mn.
Stern-Volmer plot for the quenching of CQDs fluorescence at various concentrations of (a) MnO2 NF and (b) MnO2 NS.

Emission spectrum of MnO2 NF@CQDs after addition of different concentrations of (a) 6-TG, (c) 6-MP and (e) Enapril. Exponential relationship between the F/F0 against the concentration of (b) 6-TG in the range 0–50 μMwith the Correlation Coefficient of 0.9961, (d) 6-MP in the range 0–50μM with the Correlation Coefficient of 0.9945 and (f) Enapril in the range 0–50μM with the Correlation Coefficient of 0.9917.

Emission spectrum of MnO2 NS@CQDs after addition of different concentrations of (a) 6-TG, (c) 6-MP and (e) Enapril. Exponential relationship between the F/F0 against the concentration of (b) 6-TG in the range 0–50μM with the Correlation Coefficient of 0.9917, (d) 6-MP in the range 0–50μM with the Correlation Coefficient of 0.9989 and (f) Enapril in the range 0–50μM with the Correlation Coefficient of 0.9909.
Proposed mechanism for fluorescence turn off–turn on of CQDs.
Comparison of F/F0 values for different biomolecules with (a) MnO2 NFs@CQDs and (b) MnO2 NSs@CQDs.
A comparative study for the performance of optical sensors for purine compounds.

head.ce:title: A sensitive turn on fluorescent probe for detection of biothiols using MnO2@carbon dots nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Thapar University, Patiala 147004, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Presently, the combination of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and metal oxide nanostructures in one frame are being considered for the sensing of purine compounds. In this work, a combined system of CQDs and MnO2 nanostructures was used for the detection of anticancer drugs, 6-Thioguanine (6-TG) and 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP). The CQDs were synthesized through microwave synthesizer and the MnO2 nanostructures (nanoflowers and nanosheets) were synthesized using facile hydrothermal technique. The CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence emission at 420nm when excited at 320nm wavelength. By combining CQDs and MnO2 nanostructures, quenching of fluorescence was observed which was attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, where CQDs act as electron donor and MnO2 act as acceptor. This fluorescence quenching behaviour disappeared on the addition of 6-TG and 6-MP due to the formation of Mn-S bond. The detection limit for 6-TG (0.015μM) and 6-MP (0.014μM) was achieved with the linear range of concentration (0–50μM) using both MnO2 nanoflowers and nanosheets. Moreover, the as-prepared fluorescence-sensing technique was successfully employed for the detection of bio-thiol group in enapril drug. Thus a facile, cost-effective and benign chemistry approach for biomolecule detection was designed.

ce:section.ce:para: Purine compounds play significant roles in human cell metabolism and are used in several cancer-treating medicines. 6-Thioguanine (6-TG) and 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) are purine compounds used for the treatment of cancer such as acute lymphoblastic leukemia [1]. However, the concern of their dose is of critical significance, that is, the under-dosage will be unsuccessful in the cure of disease and quite adverse effects like bone marrow suppression and liver problems may be caused by their over-dosage [2]. Therefore, sensors for detection of 6-TG and 6-MP have attracted considerable attention over the last few years. Recently, numerous techniques have been developed for the detection of these purine compounds such as fluorescence [3,2], high performance liquid chromatography [4–8], electrochemical methods [9–11], surface-plasmon resonance [12] etc. Most of these techniques have some disadvantages of being tedious, labor-intensive, sophisticated instrumentation or costly reagents, which limit their practical applications [13].

However, now a day's fluorescence sensors have been successfully developed for the detection of some analytes [14]. Fluorescent CQDs are small carbon nanoparticles (<10nm) with some form of surface passivation. These are highly stable, good conductors, highly soluble in water, photostable, biocompatible and have low toxicity along with strong and tunable fluorescent emission properties [15–17]. CQDs have promising applications in a wide range of fields of bio-medicine, optronics, catalysis, and sensing as a green alternative for the toxic transition-metal quantum dots. These attractive superiorities of CQDs make them ideal chem.-/bio-sensors at bio-imaging platforms [18,19].

The arrangement of fluorescent materials and other functional nanomaterials in one matrix have been broadly employed in the assembly of efficient sensors. Nanomaterials/semi-conductors, such as graphene oxide [20,21], molybdenum sulphide [22], gold nanoparticles [23], and silver nanoparticles [24] are good fluorescence-quenchers and have already been employed to improve fluorescent sensors for sensitive recognition of various analytes [24–26]. Manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets (NS) and nanoflowers (NF) have high specific surface area and relatively better light-absorption and fluorescence-quenching capabilities. They have attracted considerable concern in emerging “turn on” fluorescence-sensing detectors [27,28]. For example, Liu et al. synthesized the MnO2 NS combined with nanoparticles (NPs), leading to a promising fluorescence-quencher for up-converted luminescence [29]. Qu et al. also fabricate carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites for the detection of glutathione (GSH) in aqueous solutions succinctly, rapidly and selectively. Zhang et al. established a relatively simple design for the synthesis of MnO2-modified C3N4 NS sandwich nanocomposites for detection of glutathione [30,12]. However, these MnO2 probe nanocomposites had comparatively lesser stability which consequently limits their usage for biomolecule detection in vitro. In present work, MnO2@CQD system has been used as a newly designed analytical tool for the efficient, selective and relatively faster detection of 6-TG and 6-MP. Also the CQDs derived from ascorbic acid are very inexpensive and eco-friendly probe.

All the reagents used were of analytical grade and used without any advanced refinement. Ascorbic acid (99%, Loba Chemie) was used without further purification. Kollicoat-IR (MW 45,000Da) was gifted from BASF which was used as stabilizing agent in the synthesis of CQDs. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and potassium chloride (KCl) purchased from CDH private limited were used to synthesize the manganese dioxide NS and NF. Ultrapure deionized water obtained from Millipore (18MΏ) was used for preparation of all reagents and cleaning of glass wares.

Water soluble CQDs were synthesized by dissolving ascorbic acid and kollicoat (1:1, w/w%) with continuous stirring. After 30min, the sample (5mL) was transferred into a glass reaction vessel and subjected to microwave heating at 130°C for 30min at 300W power with a Multiwave-3000 microwave reaction system (Anton Paar, USA). The formation of CQDs was confirmed with the change in color of sample from colorless to pale yellow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MnO2 NS and NF
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal method was used to prepare the MnO2 NF and NS. At first, KMnO4 and KCl were dissolved in 40mL distilled water in different ratios (n(KMnO4):n(KCl)=3:1 and 1:3). The aqueous solutions were stirred continuously for 10min to mix at room temperature. A 50mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was taken and the resultant solution was transferred into it followed by heating to 160°C for 12h. The autoclave was eventually cooled down to the room temperature. The dark precipitates finally obtained were used after washing with distilled water and ethanol thoroughly and drying at 60°C for 12h.

Electronic excitations of CQDs and MnO2 NF and NS were analyzed through a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena, Specord, India) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS-55, USA) using an excitation wavelength ranging from 260 to 320nm. The XPS spectra of the catalysts were measured using XPS spectrometer (KRATOS Axis 165, Shimadzu, UK) with Mg Kα radiation (1253.6eV) at 75W. The morphology and composition of MnO2 NF/NS were analyzed by scanning electron microscope, transmission electron microscope and Oxford INCTA energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometer. Phase identification was made with a X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical X'Pert PRO, India) using Cu Kα (λ=1.540Å) as the radiation source. Particle size distribution and zeta potential was determined by dynamic light scattering method (DLS, Zetasizer, Malvern). The surface area of the NS and NF were measured through BET surface area analyser of BEL-miniII, Microtrac Corp. Pvt. Ltd., Japan.

For the detection of 6-TG and 6-MP, different concentrations of respective moieties were mixed with 46mM MnO2@CQD solution in a series of 3mL glass curettes. The fluorescence spectra were obtained after shaking for 1min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and MnO2 NF and NS
Fluorescence Turn-off of CQDs by MnO2 Nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para:
The confirmation for the reduction of MnO2 by CQDs was observed by the fluorescence quenching. Initially the CQDs showed very high fluorescence which is attributed to the radiative recombination of electrons and to the confinement of holes over the surface of these CQDs. By the interaction of CQDs with MnO2, the electrons and holes are trapped and thus, they become less available for the radiative recombination which ultimately leads to PL quenching. With the increasing concentration of MnO2 NF and NS, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreases considerably, demonstrating an efficient quenching procedure (Fig. S2 in supporting information). The average lifetime of CQDs was also to be 3.5ns which decreased to 2.9ns (Fig. S5) after the formation of MnO2@CQDs composites which confirms the quenching of CQDs. Fluorescence quenching data are usually represented by Stern-Volmer equation which gives the plot between F0/F vs. concentration of the quencher and is represented as:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q

Here, ‘F’ and ‘F0’ are the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence and absence of quencher [Q], ‘KSV’ is the Stern-Volmer constant (M−1) [33]. The plot is represented in Fig. 6(a) and Fig. 6(b) and the corresponding value of KSV is calculated to be 2.8×10−4 M−1 for NF and 1.9×10−4 M−1 for NS indicating high quenching efficiency.

Before carrying out the practical application for the sensing of 6-TG and 6-MP, the stability of CQDs was done by the UV (125W Hg arc) and visible light (CFL 60W) irradiation for 60min and no change was observed in the fluorescence intensity, signifying the good photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic Study (Testing at Different Time Intervals)
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic study was performed for MnO2 NF and NS up to the incubation time of 12h as shown in Fig. S3. 12h was selected as optimum time for the interaction of MnO2@NF and NS with CQDs which remains stable afterwards. From this investigation it is observed that, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly with increase in the incubation time.

On addition of 6-TG on MnO2@CQD probe, both MnO2 NF and NS exhibit turn-on behaviour in fluorescence of CQD. There was no change in the fluorescence intensity and position of the peak for CQDs on addition of 6-TG to the pure CQDs solution, which indicates that two of these did not interact. The MnO2 NF@CQDs and NS@CQDs exhibit fluorescence turn-on property on addition of 20μL 6-TG at each time interval as shown in Fig. 7(a) & Fig. 8(a) respectively. This indicates that even with the small addition (20μL) of 6-TG, significant change in the fluorescence intensity was observed. An exponential relationship between the F/F0 against the concentration of 6-TG was also obtained in the range of 0–50μM with the correlation coefficient of 0.9961 with MnO2 NF (Fig. 7(b)) and 0.9917 with MnO2 NS (Fig. 8(b)). This fluorescence turn-on behaviour was due to the interaction of 6-TG with MnO2 nanostructures via MnS bond, which leads to release of CQDs from MnO2@CQDs nanocomposites. The detection limit (3σ/s) for 6-TG was found to be 0.015μM with NF and 0.014μM for NS, where ‘σ’ represents the standard deviation of five blank measurements, and ‘s’ is the slope of the calibration curve.

Similarly following the above procedure, the MnO2 NF@CQDs and NS@CQDs exhibit fluorescence turn-on property on addition of 20μL 6-MP at each time as shown in Fig. 7(c) and Fig. 8(c) respectively. An exponential relationship between the F/F0 against the concentration of 6-MP was obtained in the range similar to that of 6-TG with the correlation coefficient of 0.9945 with MnO2@NF (Fig. 7(d)) and 0.9989 with MnO2@NS (Fig. 8(d)). The detection limit (3σ/s) for 6-MP was found to be 0.014μM with NF and 0.012μM for NS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for Fluorescence Turn on Detection of 6-TG and 6-MP by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2(d), the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was quenched by 93% with the interaction of MnO2 forming MnO2 NF@CQDs and NS@CQDs. The quenching of fluorescence exhibited by CQDs due to MnO2-nanostructures is mainly plausible because of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomenon [34]. According to the Förster's energy transfer theory, the energy transfer efficiency for a FRET system depends on the spectral overlap and the distance between the donor and acceptor. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs (λem =320nm) overlap with the absorption spectrum of MnO2-nanostructures (λmax =275nm). As inferred from the observations, a FRET mechanism is likely to occur in the MnO2@CQDs probe where CQDs act as donor and MnO2-nanostructures act as acceptor [35]. Consequently, effective quenching of CQDs' fluorescence takes place. However, on addition of 6-TG and 6-MP, the MnS bond is expected to form which leads to regaining of fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The MnO2@CQDs composite was stabilized by the hydroxyl and carboxyl group present on the surface of CQDs. With the addition of biomolecule, a ligand exchange reaction takes place between CQDs and biomolecule on MnO2 surface, as thiols are known to have higher affinity for metal surfaces. This leads to the release of CQDs from the MnO2 surface. As a result, the spectral overlap of the emission spectrum of CQDs and absorption spectrum of MnO2 becomes less significant, which also contributes to enhancement in fluorescence due to inefficient FRET. The proposed mechanism for this method is displayed in Fig. 9 .

In present article, we have successfully demonstrated the biosensor for the turn on fluorescence of CQDs. The CQDs were synthesized by the simplistic and cost-effective approach using ascorbic acid as precursor. The interaction of MnO2 with CQDs leads to fluorescence quenching due to FRET mechanism where CQDs act as donor and MnO2 nanostructures act as acceptor. The fluorescence intensity was regained by using 6-TG/5-MP via formation of MnS bond. A thorough analysis into the development of fluorescence turn on of CQDs was done for enapril tablets which furnished positive response. This assay was also investigated for different other biomolecules to increase its utility in pharmaceutical industries. The proposed mechanism for the turn on fluorescence of CQDs was also elaborated in this work.
The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Thapar University for providing the instrumental facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.11.041.
